findings.title: snapshot-picks.js calls Runner.evaluateStock() three times per stock (once per mode)
findings.description: pickStockForMode() invokes Runner.evaluateStock(stock) inside a per-mode loop. With 3 modes (HYPERGROWTH, QUALITY_COMPOUNDER, TURNAROUND) and ~12k tickers, every stock is fully evaluated 3 times against ~40 methods.
findings.mechanism: Outer loop `for (const modeId of ['HYPERGROWTH', 'QUALITY_COMPOUNDER', 'TURNAROUND'])` iterates 3x; inside, `for (const stock of stocks)` calls pickStockForMode(stock, modeId) which always re-runs Runner.evaluateStock(stock). The result is identical across all three modes — only SM.evaluateMode() differs. This wastes ~2/3 of the total method-evaluation CPU.
findings.reproducer: Time `node snapshot-picks.js` and measure CPU; profile shows Runner.evaluateStock running ~36k times instead of ~12k.
findings.impact: Roughly 3x runtime for the entire snapshot-picks step (~14k tickers × 3 modes × 40 methods = ~1.7M method invocations vs the optimal ~480k). In CI this is multiple minutes of wall time wasted per workflow run.
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist the evaluation: run `const allResults = {}; for (const s of stocks) { try { allResults[s.meta.ticker] = Runner.evaluateStock(s); } catch (e) { ... } }` once, then pass allResults[ticker] into SM.evaluateMode inside the per-mode loop. Mirrors the pattern already used by generate-modes-report.js evaluateAll().
findings.compound_hint: If F-PF-002 (generate-modes-report eligible/topByMethod re-evaluation) is fixed first, this fix can re-use the same shared pattern.

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: topByMethod() and topAllMust() call SM.evaluateMode again for every (mode, stock) pair, ignoring modeEvals cache
findings.description: evaluateAll() pre-computes ev.modeEvals[mId] for every stock × mode (line 92-96). But topByMethod() at line 202 and topAllMust() at line 219 then call SM.evaluateMode(ev.stock, modeId, ev.allResults) again for every eligible stock. This duplicates the entire mode-evaluation work — including the ScoreAggregator.computeScore step.
findings.mechanism: Line 202: `const me = SM.evaluateMode(ev.stock, modeId, ev.allResults);` inside the filter callback. Line 219: same, in topAllMust. The pre-computed `ev.modeEvals[modeId]` already contains `passed`, `mustResults`, `score`, etc. With 3 modes × N sub-tabs × ~12k eligible stocks, this re-runs evaluateMode ~3 × (subtabs+1) × ~12k times.
findings.reproducer: Add `let evalModeCalls = 0;` then wrap SM.evaluateMode in a counter; run `node generate-modes-report.js`. Expect counts much higher than `evaluated.length × modes.length`.
findings.impact: ScoreAggregator.computeScore is the most expensive part of evaluateMode. Wasting it on every method sub-tab adds ~5-10s of CPU per report generation that is paid in CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `const me = SM.evaluateMode(ev.stock, modeId, ev.allResults);` with `const me = ev.modeEvals && ev.modeEvals[modeId];` and proceed only if me exists. modeEvals is always populated by evaluateAll() before these functions run.
findings.compound_hint: Same memo pattern needs applying in snapshot-picks.js (F-PF-001).

findings.title: walk-forward-perf priceAt() is O(N) linear scan and runs O(vintages × horizons × tickers) times
findings.description: priceAt() does a linear `for (const entry of series)` scan over each ticker's full price-history series for every call. evaluateVintage calls it for every (pick × horizon) AND computeUniverseMedianReturn calls it for every (evaluatedTicker × horizon) AND computeFrozenVintageMedianReturn calls it for every (tickersAtVintage × horizon).
findings.mechanism: With ~12k evaluatedTickers × 3 horizons × N vintages × avg-series-length (say 500 trading days) × 2 lookups (p0 and p1) per call, this is ~36M iterations per vintage. computeUniverseMedianReturn runs once per (vintage × horizon × mode) — and is called inside the mode loop on line 176 so it's recomputed 3x per horizon per vintage (same data!).
findings.reproducer: Run `node scripts/walk-forward-perf.js` against current picks-history (~6 vintages) and prices/history.json. Each universe-median call is repeated 3 times per horizon (once per mode) producing identical results.
findings.impact: On a year of vintages (~50 weekly vintages) walk-forward-perf becomes a multi-minute job; with the 3-mode duplication, ~67% is wasted work.
findings.suggested_fix: Two fixes: (a) Build an index per series: when first touched, sort or already-sorted series can have a Map<isoDate, close> for O(1) lookups, or use binary search since dates are sorted. (b) Hoist computeUniverseMedianReturn out of the per-mode loop — it does not depend on mode, only on (asOf, horizon, evaluatedTickers). Compute once per (vintage × horizon) and reuse for all 3 modes. Same for frozenVintage and SPY.
findings.compound_hint: method-effectiveness.js (F-PF-004) reuses WF.priceAt with even worse multipliers, so fixing priceAt indexes both.

findings.title: method-effectiveness.js: O(vintages × horizons × tickers × methods) priceAt calls; price lookup repeated per method
findings.description: Inner loop calls WF.priceAt(prices, ticker, asOf) and WF.priceAt(prices, ticker, futureDate) per ticker per horizon — correct. But then iterates Object.entries(stockData.results) which has ~40 method ids; the return value `ret` is correctly hoisted, but for each new methods-history file (~14MB JSON) and ~12k tickers × ~40 methods × 2 horizons = ~960k inner iterations per vintage.
findings.mechanism: While `ret` is already computed once per ticker × horizon (good), priceAt itself is still the O(N) linear scan inherited from walk-forward-perf. With methods-history files growing from 6MB to 14MB and more vintages accumulating daily, runtime scales quadratically: more vintages × more tickers per vintage × longer price series.
findings.reproducer: Run `node scripts/method-effectiveness.js` and measure runtime as files in methods-history/ accumulate. Adding fixture vintages should show roughly linear growth per vintage, but with a large constant due to the O(N) priceAt.
findings.impact: Once methods-history grows past ~30 vintages (a few months), this script will exceed CI step limits and start timing out. The bootstrap CI (200 resamples) is small change compared to priceAt cost.
findings.suggested_fix: Same as F-PF-003: build a per-ticker Map<isoDate, close> once at startup (or use binary search on the sorted series). Additionally, hoist `(p0_ticker, p1_ticker)` per horizon so each ticker is priced once per horizon and reused across all ~40 methods.
findings.compound_hint: Fixing priceAt in walk-forward-perf.js (where WF is exported from) fixes both scripts simultaneously.

findings.title: snapshot-methods-history.js writes 14MB JSON with JSON.stringify(data, null, 2) — pretty-print 5-10x slowdown and disk bloat
findings.description: Output file ~14MB per day stored with 2-space indentation. JSON.stringify with indent argument is significantly slower than compact stringify and bloats the file ~30-40%, costing CI time on both write and subsequent reads by method-effectiveness.js (which re-parses all of them).
findings.mechanism: `fs.writeFileSync(outFile, JSON.stringify(data, null, 2))` — the indent argument forces a slower formatter path. Confirmed file sizes 6-14MB; without indent these would be ~9-10MB and parse/stringify ~2-3x faster.
findings.reproducer: Compare `JSON.stringify(data, null, 2).length` vs `JSON.stringify(data).length` and time both for a representative methods-history file.
findings.impact: Daily ~14MB git commit (vs ~9MB), slower CI step, more memory churn during method-effectiveness.js loadJson. Compounds over time as history grows.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `null, 2` indent — these files are machine-read only, never hand-edited. Either drop the argument entirely, or guard behind an env flag for debugging.
findings.compound_hint: Same issue exists in snapshot-picks.js line 195/197, generate-modes-report.js line 1259, and elsewhere — a project-wide pass.

findings.title: generate-modes-report renders embedded JSON for every card via data-stock attribute (massive HTML bloat)
findings.description: Each card's data-stock attribute serializes `stockSlim` (including timeseries.revenueQ × 8, annual arrays × 5, etc.) into the HTML using escHtml(JSON.stringify(...)). With multiple modes × 200 picks × 4 tier sections + near-misses, this produces hundreds of KB of redundant inline JSON.
findings.mechanism: renderCard is called O(modes × topN × tier-groups) — and each card embeds a ~1-2KB JSON payload. escHtml double-escapes every character. The same stock can appear in multiple modes' card-grids, duplicating its slim payload.
findings.reproducer: Generate the report and observe HTML size; check how many times the same ticker's data-stock payload appears (often 2-3x across modes).
findings.impact: Larger HTML = slower page load and slower buildHtml() string construction (the join() of all these strings allocates heavily).
findings.suggested_fix: Build a single window.STOCKS = {ticker: stockSlim} JSON blob once at the top of the page, and have cards reference by ticker only (data-ticker). The modal opens by looking up STOCKS[ticker]. Trims HTML by 50%+ for multi-mode pages.
findings.compound_hint: buildHtml uses chained string concatenation/template-literals — switching to an array of fragments joined at the end would also help.

findings.title: loadStocks reads and JSON.parses ~12k snapshot files serially with fs.readFileSync inside a map()
findings.description: loadStocks does `fs.readdirSync(dir)` then `files.map(f => JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(...)))` — all sync, single-threaded. ~12k files × O(disk-seek+parse) accumulates seconds even on warm cache. snapshot-picks.js and snapshot-methods-history.js have parallel patterns.
findings.mechanism: Each readFileSync blocks the event loop; with 12k files we get 12k syscalls plus 12k JSON.parse calls on payloads up to 30KB each. No parallelism possible with sync I/O. Even on SSD this is the largest constant in script startup.
findings.reproducer: Time `node -e "require('./generate-modes-report.js')"` startup vs the actual evaluation work — load phase usually dominates.
findings.impact: ~5-15s per script just for snapshot loading × multiple scripts in the pipeline = wasted minutes per CI run.
findings.suggested_fix: Switch to async fs.promises.readFile with a p-limit-style pool of ~32 concurrent reads (or use the worker-pool pattern from pull-yahoo.js). Or alternatively, write all snapshots as a single combined snapshots.json after pull-yahoo and load that one file.
findings.compound_hint: snapshot-picks.js loadStocks (line 24-35), snapshot-methods-history.js (line 29-91), methods/sector-medians-compute.js (line 234-238) all repeat the pattern.

findings.title: snapshot-picks _buildFirstSeenMap reads and parses every prior picks-history vintage on every run
findings.description: _buildFirstSeenMap iterates every YYYY-MM-DD.json in picks-history/, JSON-parses each (~50-60KB), then walks every mode's picks. Files grow indefinitely as vintages accumulate; this O(vintages × picks) cost is paid on every run just to derive a small ticker→date map.
findings.mechanism: After 1 year of weekly picks (~52 files × 60KB = ~3MB), this loop dominates startup. Each subsequent vintage adds linear cost. The map could be derived incrementally — once a ticker has a firstSeenAt, it never changes.
findings.reproducer: Synthetically populate picks-history with 100+ vintages and time snapshot-picks.js. Compare to a single-file run.
findings.impact: Modest today (~6 vintages), but compounding. By month 12 this is several seconds; by year 3 it could exceed 30s.
findings.suggested_fix: Persist a derived `picks-history/_first-seen.json` { mode: { ticker: 'YYYY-MM-DD' } } and update it incrementally each run (only merge today's new tickers). Rebuild fully only if the cache is missing.
findings.compound_hint: Same scan-all-history pattern in scripts/method-effectiveness.js (parses every 14MB methods-history file every run) — F-PF-009.

findings.title: method-effectiveness.js JSON.parses every methods-history file (~14MB each) on every CI run
findings.description: `const file = loadJson(path.join(METHODS_HIST_DIR, fname));` is called for every vintage. Each file is ~14MB. With even 30 vintages, that's ~420MB of JSON to parse and hold in memory just to extract pass/fail flags.
findings.mechanism: loadJson uses fs.readFileSync → JSON.parse — Node's parser is single-threaded and synchronous. Worse, the entire object stays in scope until the inner for-loop completes, so peak memory grows with vintage count. No streaming.
findings.reproducer: Run with NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=2048 against a year of accumulated history. Watch RSS climb.
findings.impact: OOM risk in CI once history exceeds ~100 vintages; large GC pause times; long script wall time even when no new data has been added (everything is recomputed from scratch every run).
findings.suggested_fix: Two improvements: (a) Persist a derived/aggregated result keyed by (vintage_date, method_id) so already-processed vintages are not re-parsed; only new vintages are read each run. (b) For new vintages, stream-parse with a JSON streaming library (or split the per-day file into smaller per-method shards at write time in snapshot-methods-history.js).
findings.compound_hint: Coupled with F-PF-005 (pretty-print bloat in those same files) — together a single fix can halve parsing time.

findings.title: pull-yahoo.js sortByStaleness reopens and reads first 300 bytes of every snapshot file synchronously
findings.description: sortByStaleness does fs.openSync + fs.readSync(300 bytes) + fs.closeSync for every ticker in the watchlist (~12k) — purely to extract the asOf timestamp. The comparator getAge is called O(n log n) times by Array.sort, meaning ~2-3 sync I/O operations per ticker can happen.
findings.mechanism: Array.prototype.sort's comparator runs on average O(n log n) times. Even with comparator-side caching missing, every ticker is read once. With 12k tickers that's 12k+ syscalls just to decide pull order. The same data is then re-read by _getExistingSnapshotAge (line 600-613) when processOne runs.
findings.reproducer: Add timing around sortByStaleness in pull-yahoo.js; expect ~1-3s wall-clock for a 12k watchlist.
findings.impact: Adds 1-3s startup latency to the longest-running workflow step. Not catastrophic but easy win.
findings.suggested_fix: Materialize ages once into a Map<ticker, ageMs> before sort (single pass over all stocks, no comparator recompute). Reuse the same map in _getExistingSnapshotAge by caching on the stock object: `stock._snapshotAgeMs = age;` during the staleness pass.
findings.compound_hint: Trivially compounds with F-PF-007 (snapshot loading) — if we maintain a snapshots-meta.json index of {ticker: asOf}, both problems disappear.

findings.title: generate-modes-report dedupeByCompany rebuilds heavy regex per call
findings.description: norm() defined inside dedupeByCompany contains a heavyweight regex `/\b(inc|corporation|corp|incorporated|...)\b/gi` recreated on every call. With ~12k stocks, that's 12k regex constructions plus 12k normalizations.
findings.mechanism: JavaScript function-scoped const re-binds the regex literal on each function invocation. Hoisting it to module scope eliminates the recreation.
findings.impact: Minor — sub-second total for the regex creation cost. Listed for completeness.
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist the regex literal to module scope as a const, e.g. `const NORM_SUFFIX_RE = /\b(inc|...)\b/gi;` Same pattern appears in snapshot-picks.js dedupePicksByCompany (line 76-81).
findings.compound_hint: Trivial cleanup; bundle with other low-priority cleanups.

findings.title: pull-yahoo.js fundamentalsTimeSeries fetched serially per ticker (4 separate awaited calls)
findings.description: fetchFundamentalsTS does 4 sequential awaits: annualFin, quarterlyFin, annualCash, annualBs. Each is its own HTTP round-trip with separate Yahoo crumb validation. Within a single ticker these calls are independent and could run in parallel via Promise.all.
findings.mechanism: Lines 400/403/406/410 each `await yf.fundamentalsTimeSeries(...)`. Each call is ~500ms-2s. The 4 calls sum to 2-8s per ticker. With 12k tickers × ~80% needing full pull, this is a multi-minute additional latency.
findings.reproducer: Time a single processOne() call against a popular ticker; observe ~4 sequential FTS network calls.
findings.impact: Pull duration is the longest workflow step; serializing what could be parallel doubles to quadruples per-ticker pull time.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each in its own try/catch and Promise.all them: `const [annualFin, quarterlyFin, annualCash, annualBs] = await Promise.all([safe(...), safe(...), safe(...), safe(...)]);` where safe() returns [] on failure. Reduces per-ticker latency by ~3-4x for the FTS portion.
findings.compound_hint: The yf concurrency queue already caps total parallel HTTP, so this won't blow rate-limit budgets.

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: buildSparkline builds inline SVG via string concat in a hot path
findings.description: buildSparkline does small string concatenations: `(x.toFixed(1) + ',' + y.toFixed(1))` in a map then joins. Cards/rows also rely on template literal concatenation. Not catastrophic but called O(modes × picks) times.
findings.mechanism: Each card renders ~200 chars of inline SVG; with up to 800 cards per page, that's ~160KB of string allocation. Template literals are fine; explicit '+ ,' + concatenation is the slowest pattern.
findings.reproducer: Profile buildHtml() generation; sparkline + card rendering dominate string allocation.
findings.impact: Minor — sub-second total. Listed because tasked.
findings.suggested_fix: If profiling shows this matters, pre-format with arr.join(',') or template literal `${x.toFixed(1)},${y.toFixed(1)}`. Otherwise leave alone.
findings.compound_hint: Lower priority than the other findings.

findings.title: loadFx and other module-scope IIFEs run fs.existsSync + readFileSync at require-time
findings.description: loadFx() runs as a require-side-effect IIFE. Combined with other require-time initialization across many scripts (Runner.METHODS loads every method file synchronously at require), CI cold-start of any script that imports the methods runner pays a non-trivial sync I/O tax.
findings.mechanism: Every require of pull-yahoo (or any script that transitively imports it) executes loadFx which reads fx-rates.json. Multiply by all the scripts in the workflow that all import similar setups.
findings.reproducer: Time `node -e "require('./pull-yahoo.js')"` vs an empty require — startup overhead is measurable.
findings.impact: Adds ~100-500ms per CI step in startup. Across ~10 scripts in a workflow, ~1-5s wasted.
findings.suggested_fix: Defer loadFx() until pullAll() is actually called, not at module-require time. Wrap in a lazy memoized loader: `function getFx() { if (_fxLoaded) return; ... }`. Same for any other module-scope IIFEs across the codebase.
findings.compound_hint: Low-impact alone but coupled with the Runner.METHODS eager load it adds up.
